title: Boardroom churn signals a corporate reset in Colombo
body: Sri Lanka’s blue chips are quietly retooling for a tougher operating climate. Hatton National Bank appointed veteran industrialist Suresh Shah as Chairman, bringing cross-sector clout from tobacco, beverages and manufacturing into banking governance. At the same time, Ceylon Beverage Holdings and Lion Brewery added seasoned finance hands to their boards, while LOLC parachuted a next‑gen tech‑savvy executive onto three plantation boards to inject data and AI‑led discipline into legacy estates. Sarvodaya Development Finance tapped a capital-markets lawyer to its board, and ACL Cables named a new Group CEO with deep investment banking pedigree. The subtext: cost, capital and cashflow now trump expansionary bravado; boards want operatives who can squeeze working‑capital cycles, refinance at thinner spreads and digitalise back‑office drudgery without missing a dividend.

title: Washington wants reciprocity as London loosens rules
body: The United States is resetting expectations. At an AmCham forum in Colombo, the US Embassy urged greater two‑way trade, pressing for higher imports of American agriculture, machinery and pharmaceuticals, and inviting outbound Sri Lankan investment into the US—all under the rubric of regulatory predictability. Britain, meanwhile, has already made a concrete move. Its revised Developing Countries Trading Scheme (DCTS) eases rules of origin, letting apparel SMEs source inputs more flexibly without losing preferences. For Sri Lanka, whose garment giants already play in the premier league, the opportunity is to pull smaller suppliers into UK buyer shortlists by cutting lead times and de‑risking fabrics. The test now is execution: credit that matches production cycles, faster customs and targeted buyer outreach will determine whether policy translates into orders.

title: Colombo hedges: energy talk with Moscow, deal hunters from Penang, and policy learning in New Delhi
body: Sri Lanka is stitching a pragmatic external cushion. Russia’s Deputy Foreign Minister said Moscow is ‘deeply considering’ supplying energy and favours longer‑term contracts—music to a country exposed to spot‑market shocks. Malaysian Indian business leaders toured the BOI and Port City to scope FDIs in ICT, renewables, data centres and agri‑processing, while the Sri Lanka–Benelux Business Council courted Dutch investors in ports, water and agritech. A parliamentary delegation studied India’s infrastructure toolkits—Gati Shakti planning, PPP port operations and power exchanges—signalling intent to import process, not just projects. Simultaneously, the envoy in Moscow advanced ties with Kazakhstan, and the ambassador in Myanmar pushed investment and labour‑protection agendas. The posture is clear: diversify risk, learn from neighbours, and convert diplomacy into pipelines, not press releases.

title: War in the Gulf tests a fragile recovery: fuel rationing restored, power costs rise and water is rationed
body: The Middle East conflict has moved from headlines to household bills. With the Strait of Hormuz constrained and shipping insurance spiking, Sri Lanka re‑imposed the QR fuel quota, warned of tighter diesel supply for generation, and lifted electricity tariffs—triggering price hikes for restaurants and everyday tea. LAUGFS landed 7,000 tonnes of LPG to shore up Avurudu cooking demand, but the National Water Board still rolled out night water cuts in Colombo suburbs to balance hydropower, irrigation and drinking needs ahead of Yala. Policymakers face ugly trade‑offs: pay more for scarce ⚡ diesel to keep evening lights on, or risk productivity‑sapping blackouts; protect the poorest from tariff pass‑throughs, or preserve fiscal targets agreed with the IMF. A joint WB–IMF–IEA mechanism to coordinate support on energy shocks will help at the margin. The strategic fix—more rooftop solar, faster grid‑scale renewables and storage—cannot.

title: Policy arguments sharpen: managing shocks, innovation and governance strains
body: A former Central Bank deputy governor warned that overlapping external shocks have shattered post‑default recovery hopes and that Sri Lanka lacks fiscal space, while monetary easing is constrained by transmission frictions. His prescription—productivity first, wage restraint and technology adoption—echoes a parallel call to redefine ‘innovation’ as solving real domestic bottlenecks (cold chains, farm logistics, SME finance), not chasing vanity apps. Governance frictions also rose. A trenchant critique of delaying provincial council polls accused the administration of ‘deep‑freezing’ devolution and warned of political overreach. In the courts, however, accountability advanced: the Court of Appeal upheld an RTI Commission order compelling disclosure of temple bank accounts, underlining that public‑trust funds aren’t private cloaks. The week’s debates point to the same conclusion: resilience lies in better institutions as much as in better balance sheets.

title: Markets wobble as a bank stumbles; the state leans harder on bills
body: Financial markets flashed mixed signals. National Development Bank disclosed an estimated LKR 4 bn quarterly hit from an internal fraud, even as it stressed capital ratios remain above regulatory minima. Equity indices oscillated on thin conviction—big blocks in JKH changed hands, but the ASPI drifted, tracking energy headlines and bank news. In fixed income, Treasury‑bill yields ticked up for a second week and auctions were undersubscribed, pushing more funding into the second tranche. The rupee eased to roughly 315.5 per dollar; bonds were broadly steady. Against this backdrop, property showed surprising animal spirits: John Keells sold 200‑plus apartments at its new Vauxhall DSTRCT within two weeks, suggesting urban buyers still back inflation‑hedging bricks and mortar when the pricing is right.

title: Digital rails thicken: cross‑border QR, person‑to‑person scan‑to‑pay and a nudge toward e‑governance
body: Payments plumbing deepened on three fronts. LankaPay enabled Weixin (WeChat) Pay acceptance at 400,000+ LANKAQR merchants, letting Chinese visitors scan‑and‑pay seamlessly—useful for tourism and a signal of EMVCo‑standard interoperability. Dialog launched ‘GanuDenu QR’, a zero‑fee person‑to‑person option up to LKR 5,000, widening everyday digital cash replacement. And the Justice Ministry lit up a new MIS and citizen portal—built under an EU‑funded project—to standardise appointments of Commissioners of Oaths and sworn translators, and improve transparency. Meanwhile, Mintpay rolled out no‑expiry digital vouchers for 2,500 merchants, and Commercial Bank became the first to add 3‑D Secure to LANKAQR/JCB e‑commerce, tightening online risk. These aren’t headline‑grabbers, but each lowers friction, formalises flows and—done at scale—nudges the economy onto faster, cheaper rails.

title: Trade cushions and supply chains: rice, metal, fertiliser and fish
body: The Cabinet approved purchases of over 61,000 tonnes of paddy for LKR 7.4 bn and green‑lit limited April imports of GR 11 rice substitutes to avert a Keeri Samba shortfall flagged by farm research agencies. Aluminium’s global price spike, driven by Gulf disruptions, has begun to seep into construction input costs. On fertiliser, a tussle over stock visibility and price caps sharpened as policymakers try to balance food security with fiscal prudence. One bright spot: a Rs. 3 bn single super phosphate plant came online, finally tapping Eppawala rock to substitute a chunk of imports. And in fisheries, Colombo Dockyard laid keel for a high‑spec cable‑lay and repair vessel—niche tonnage that earns hard currency and underlines the strategic value of maritime services.

title: Civil society pushes back as geopolitics intrudes
body: The Sri Lanka Committee for Solidarity with Palestine condemned Israel’s new death‑penalty law that targets only Palestinians in military courts, calling it discriminatory and unlawful under international humanitarian law. Domestically, Easter‑attack narratives remained contested; a new book drew legal‑process challenges and political heat even as the Bar Council leadership turned over. RTI activists marked International Fact‑Checking Day warning of AI‑accelerated disinformation, and the Court of Appeal’s RTI ruling reinforced the primacy of transparency over convenient secrecy. In parallel, a UN Women–backed Country Gender Equality Profile documented Sri Lanka’s steep slide in the Global Gender Gap rankings, arguing that unlocking labour‑force participation is as much about institutions and safety as it is about jobs. The through‑line: rights and credibility are economic variables, not just moral ones.

title: Tourism and retail find their footing as Avurudu looms
body: Despite higher utility costs, consumer‑facing businesses are leaning into the festive window. Havelock City Mall rolled out a ‘Nostalgic Avurudu’ programme to drive footfall, while SPAR opened in Kandy and Kurunegala to extend modern trade beyond the Western Province. Shangri‑La Hambantota hosted a multi‑day cross‑border wedding, pointing to the revival of big‑ticket destination events that activate wide local value chains. On the services side, banks and telcos are sweetening card rewards and micro‑vouchers to stimulate spend without deep discounting. The sector’s constraint is still cost‑push inflation from ⚡ tariffs and freight—not demand per se. If fuel remains available and skies stay open, the seasonal uptick should hold.
